2025–26 Program Evaluation
Overview By Question By School Garden Attitudes Lessons & Learning Methodology Next Steps

2025–26 Student Learning Evaluation

Did the program work? Here's what the data says.

739 Pre-Survey Students 601 Post-Survey Students 16 Schools Analyzed 22 Questions
Program Overview

What We Measured & What We Found

At the start and end of each school year, all 4th grade classrooms at each of our schools recieve the same 22-question survey covering knowledge, skills, behaviors, and attitudes related to food, water, ecology, waste, and the garden. We compared pre- and post-survey results across all schools, regardless of whether or how much the class utilized the garden, creating a random sample. Our evaluation asks: did our program make real change?

Note: Of the 33 schools where we work, only 20 responded at all to either test. Four of these responding schools were excluded from this analysis: Nipomo and San Gabriel did not complete post-tests. Templeton and Georgia Brown only had 1 to 4 students respond at all.

11
Questions with significant gains
out of 22 questions
+19pp
Biggest single-question gain
Water efficiency knowledge
4
Schools with significant overall gains
Virginia Peterson, Sinsheimer, Pat Butler, Vineyard
16
Schools in the analysis
≥10 students completing both surveys
How to read "percentage points" (pp): If 44% of students answered correctly before the program and 63% answered correctly after, that's a +19 percentage point gain. We use this instead of "percent change" because it's more direct and easier to interpret. Also keep in mind that 1% represents about 100 students across our 10,000+ student cohort.
What does "statistically significant" mean? When we say a result is "significant," it means the change we observed is very unlikely to be due to random chance — there is less than a 5% probability the result happened by accident. It doesn't necessarily mean the change was huge, but it does mean it was real and not just noise in the data.

Change in Correct Responses: All 22 Questions

Green = statistically significant improvement  |  Red = significant decline  |  Gray = no significant change  |  Hover for details

Detailed Results

Results by Question

Filter by topic or click column headers to sort. "Pre %" = share of students who answered correctly or positively before the program. "Post %" = share of students who answered correctly after the program. Click column headers to sort. "n" represents the number of students who responded to each question on the Pre or Post test. "p-Value" is related to a question's statistical significance. "Effect" indicates how large a change is with >.2 = small, >.5 = medium, and >.8 = large.

School-by-School Results

How Did Each School Do?

We compared each school's overall average score on pre- and post-surveys, averaged across all 22 questions. Only schools with at least 10 students completing both surveys are included.

Important caveat: Surveys are anonymous — although we do record which school and teacher survey responses were coming from, we cannot match the same student's pre and post responses. Developing a student code system would require more effort but would produce more informative results.
Outliers: The best performing and worst performing school have at least one thing in common--only about half as many students took the post-test as the pre-test. This could lead to biasing of the data if students who didn't respond had more or less garden exposure than students who did.

Pre vs. Post Scores by School

Blue = pre-survey average  |  Dark green = significant post gain  |  Light green = non-significant post  |  Red = approaching decline

Are Schools Statistically Different From Each Other?

One-way ANOVA tested whether school-to-school variation in scores is greater than chance would predict.

p < .001
Pre-survey scores differ significantly across schools (F = 3.09)
p < .001
Post-survey scores also differ significantly (F = 4.41, η² = 0.10)
What this means in plain language: Schools genuinely differ from each other in both starting knowledge and end-of-year outcomes. School context — teacher engagement, prior garden experience, grade levels served — matters. The program doesn't produce identical results everywhere, which is expected and worth exploring in each school's local context.
A Closer Look

Why Did Garden Attitude Scores Decline?

Three garden attitude questions showed declines at the end of the year, warranting deeper exploration of the underlying causes.

"My favorite place at school is the garden"
−6.7pp  |  p = .013 (significant)

Fewer students chose the garden as their favorite place by year-end. Novelty may fade, or competing preferences (recess, library) reassert themselves over time. Students are only allowed to choose one favorite place--a ranked choice might be more accurate. Social and cultural stigmas against gardening might also be at play as students mature.
"I feel happy in the garden" (0–4 scale)
−0.19 points  |  p = .002 (significant)

Average happiness dropped slightly — but post-survey scores were more variable, meaning some students loved it more while others liked it less. The program may produce divergent garden attitudes rather than uniformly lifting them.
"I look forward to school on garden days"
−4.4pp  |  p = .090 (not significant)

This approached significance but didn't cross the threshold. The direction is consistent with the others — worth monitoring in future years.
One hypothesis worth exploring: Students who take the pre-survey are new to the garden and naturally curious. If pre-surveys are administered soon after fun, engaging garden tours, students may have a recency bias in their question responses. Also, by year-end, some have had mixed experiences — difficult tasks, hot weather, challenging lessons. Attitudes grounded in real experience are more authentic than initial excitement. Consider using teacher feedback or small focus groups to explore what's driving this in specific schools.
Lessons Taught & Learning Outcomes

Do More Lessons = More Learning?

One of the most natural questions to ask is whether schools that received more lessons showed bigger improvements on the survey. We matched lesson counts from the 2025–26 Impact Report to pre-to-post score change across all 16 schools with sufficient survey responses. The answer is nuanced.

−0.45
Pearson Correlation (r)
p = .08 — not statistically significant
−0.54
Spearman Rank Correlation (ρ)
p = .030 — statistically significant
16
Schools Analyzed
All with pre, post & lesson data
Negative
Direction of Trend
More lessons → smaller measured gains
What this means: Both statistical methods point the same direction — schools with more lessons tended to show smaller pre-to-post score gains. The Spearman result crosses the significance threshold (p = .030), meaning this pattern is unlikely due to chance. However, the most likely explanation is a ceiling effect: schools receiving more lessons already start with higher baseline knowledge, leaving less room for the survey to detect improvement.

Lessons Taught vs. Pre-to-Post Score Change (2025–26)

Each circle = one school. Size reflects post-survey student count. Hover for details. Green = statistically significant gain  |  Red = statistically significant decline  |  Gray = no significant change  |  Dashed line = trend

Significant gain (p < .05) Significant decline (p < .05) No significant change Trend line

Baseline Knowledge by Lesson Volume

Schools grouped into three bands. The blue bars show how much students already knew before the year began. Higher starting knowledge = less room to show survey gains.

Most likely explanation: ceiling effect. Schools receiving 150+ lessons averaged a 77.9% pre-survey score — already above schools receiving under 90 lessons (73.5%). When students start the year knowing more, the survey has less room to detect gains. This is not evidence that lessons are ineffective.
The 4 schools with significant gains had 65–156 lessons. Virginia Peterson (+14.9pp), Sinsheimer (+9.6pp), Pat Butler (+8.3pp), and Vineyard (+7.4pp) had moderate lesson counts — but three of the four started with lower baselines (67–74%), giving the survey more room to show real learning.
What this analysis cannot tell us. Lesson counts are school-level, not classroom-level. A school with 261 lessons may have distributed them unevenly. Adding a teacher name drop-down to the survey would allow us to isolate lesson dosage as a variable in future years.
How We Analyzed the Data

Methodology — Plain Language

We want to be transparent about how these results were produced, what choices were made, and where the limitations are.

📋

The surveys

Students completed the same 22-question survey at the start (pre) and end (post) of the school year. 739 students completed pre; 601 completed post. These are different anonymous groups — the same individual student cannot be tracked across both surveys.

🔢

Scoring answers

Each question was assigned a pre-coded numeric score. Binary questions (right/wrong, yes/no) score 0 or 1. "I feel happy in the garden" uses a 0–4 scale (Almost never to Almost always). "I save water by…" counts how many water-saving actions were selected (1–4).

🔲

Blank answers

Students who skipped a question are excluded from that question's analysis only — they are NOT given a zero. Giving a zero to a blank would unfairly penalize students who may not have seen or understood the question. Blank counts ranged from 2 to 47 per question.

📊

The statistical test: Welch's t-test

We used Welch's independent-samples t-test for every question. "Independent-samples" because pre and post are two separate anonymous groups. "Welch's" (not Student's) because our groups have different sizes and unequal variance — confirmed by Levene's test for 16 of 22 questions. Welch's automatically adjusts for this.

📏

Effect size (Cohen's d)

A p-value tells you whether a result is real. Cohen's d tells you how big it is. Our significant results fall in the "small" range (d = 0.13–0.39). In education research, small effects consistently observed across hundreds of students are meaningful — they don't mean the program isn't working.

⚠️

Key limitation

Without a control group (schools not receiving the program), we cannot prove the program caused the changes. Some change might have happened regardless due to maturation, season, or other school activities. A waitlist-control design in a future year would strengthen causal claims significantly.

Looking Ahead

Next Steps & Recommendations

The 2025–26 evaluation gives us a strong foundation. These five recommendations would meaningfully strengthen what we can learn and claim from next year's data onward.

1

Establish a Control Group

The single biggest improvement we could make to this evaluation is adding a comparison school — ideally a school without a garden that hasn't recently had one, and whose teachers haven't participated in OCE programming. Having a control group would allow us to separate the program's effect from factors that change over time for all students regardless (like maturation, season, or broader school curriculum).

Compensating the control school: This is not only acceptable but standard practice in educational research. Offering waitlist priority (first in line to receive the full program the following year) or free add-ons (field trips, materials, family cooking nights) is a well-established and ethical way to thank a school for participating as a control. It ensures no school is permanently disadvantaged by being in the comparison group, and it builds goodwill and a pipeline of future partners.
2

Add a Teacher Name Drop-Down to the Survey

Currently, students type their teacher's name as free text, resulting in dozens of spelling variations for the same person — making it nearly impossible to group responses by class. Replacing this with a pre-populated drop-down for each school's teacher roster would unlock several new analyses:

  • Compare learning outcomes class-by-class within the same school
  • Correlate whether a teacher signed up for OCE lessons with their students' scores
  • Identify which classrooms are thriving and which may need additional support
  • Track individual teacher engagement over multiple years
  • Identify educator impacts
3

Investigate the Decline in Garden Attitude Scores

Three garden attitude questions declined significantly at the end of the year — a pattern worth understanding before drawing conclusions. Possible next steps include:

  • Ask teachers — do end-of-year surveys feel rushed or perfunctory to students?
  • Discuss internally — does the team notice a shift in student energy in the second half of the year?
  • Interview students — small focus groups at 2–3 schools could surface qualitative reasons for the shift
  • Check survey timing — pre-surveys should ideally be administered before garden tours to prevent recency bias. Post-surveys administered during testing season or near summer break may reflect survey fatigue rather than genuine attitude change
4

Review Question Relevance for 4th Grade

Several questions already have very high pre-survey scores — for example, "Why eat a rainbow of foods?" was answered correctly by 87% of students before the program began, leaving almost no room to show improvement. A curriculum review should ask: are these questions still appropriately challenging for 4th graders? Are they taught in our lessons still, at that grade level?

Questions to examine: "Why eat a rainbow of foods?" (87% pre), "Where does paper go?" (83% pre), and "Which provides more habitat?" (78% pre) all showed near-zero change — likely because they were already at or near ceiling. Replacing them with more discriminating questions would give the evaluation more room to detect real learning.
5

Keep the Survey as Consistent as Possible Year Over Year

This evaluation already spans four years of data (2022–23 through 2025–26), which is genuinely valuable, although early evaluations were much different than today's evaluation — multi-year trend data is rare in garden education research. Changing questions makes it impossible to compare results across years, erasing that accumulated record. The recommendation is to treat the survey as a stable instrument: if a question needs to be retired (see #4 above), replace it once with a clearly better version and then hold it steady.

One Cool Earth — A Place to Grow
